text: The surgeon completes dissection on the medial ipsilateral posterior sheath and prepares to identify the location for starting the crossover.
The surgeon angles the scissors upward towards the linea alba to locate the correct dissection plane, facilitating the crossover procedure.
The surgeon slowly dissects across the surgical field, aiming to identify the contralateral posterior sheath. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assist in visualizing the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon gently dissects across from the ipsilateral to the contralateral side, closely following the linea alba to maintain alignment with the anatomical plane.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence, visible as a hint of green, to identify the interface between the dissected tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the contralateral posterior sheath, highlighted by a hint of green from the ICG dye, indicating the interface location.

The surgeon begins dissecting the hernia sac on the ipsilateral side, using a quick peak port in the left upper quadrant for decompression if needed. The hernia is carefully dissected before proceeding to the contralateral side.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to clearly delineate the medial side of the contralateral posterior sheath, highlighting the interface between anatomical structures.

The surgeon begins dissection at the interface, gently pushing off infused tissue on the contralateral side, which is delineated with ICG. The pre-established space from the e-TAPs method is accessed from the ipsilateral side.
The surgeon identifies a segment of the retro-rectus space using ICG, which highlights the specific area for dissection.
The video shows the interface between the ICG-infused tissue and the normal tissue on the right side, highlighting the contrast between the two tissue types.
